dialogue.text: how can I click on cell B16 to run a macro

As an AI language model, I don't know the exact details of your macro or what program you are using. However, here's a general guide on how to link a macro to a specific cell:

1. Open the program where you created the macro (such as Microsoft Excel)
2. Open the worksheet where your macro is located
3. Select the cell (B16) where you want to link the macro
4. Go to the "Developer" tab on the ribbon (if you don't see it, you need to enable it)
5. Click on the "Insert" button to create a new button or shape
6. Right-click the newly inserted button or shape and select "Assign Macro"
7. Choose the macro you want to link to the cell (B16)
8. Click "OK"
9. Test your macro by clicking on the cell (B16) or the button/shape you linked it to.

Note: The steps might vary slightly depending on the program and version you are using.
how can I call a macro from cell B16 without using a button

You can call a macro from cell B16 by using a worksheet event. Here's how you can do it:

1. Open the worksheet where you want to call the macro.
2. Press Alt + F11 to open the VBA editor.
3. In the VBA editor, double-click on the worksheet where you want to call the macro. This will open a new code window for the worksheet.
4. In the code window, select Worksheet from the left drop-down list and then select the SelectionChange event from the right drop-down list.
5. Add the following code to the SelectionChange event:

```
Private Sub Worksheet_SelectionChange(ByVal Target As Range)
    If Target.Address = "$B$16" Then
        'Call your macro here
        MacroName
    End If
End Sub
```

6. Replace "MacroName" with the name of the macro you want to call.
7. Save the workbook and close the VBA editor.
8. Now, whenever you select cell B16, the macro specified in the code will be executed.

Note: This code will only work if you select cell B16 by clicking on it or moving to it using the arrow keys. If you navigate to cell B16 using other methods (such as a hyperlink), the macro will not be executed.
Please correct; ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:B34").ClearContents

There doesn't seem to be anything wrong with the code you provided. 

This code clears the contents of cells B17 to B34 on the worksheet named "Start Page" in the workbook where the macro code is running.
B17 is merged with C17, same all the way to B34. could this be the cause of the error

Yes, merging cells can sometimes cause problems when clearing contents using VBA. 

Instead of using `ClearContents`, you can use `Clear` to remove the merged cells entirely. Here's an updated code that should work:

```
ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:C34").Clear
```

This code will clear the merged cells B17:C34 along with any contents or formatting within it.
I do not want to remove the merged cells

If you don't want to remove the merged cells entirely, then you can try using the `UnMerge` method before using `ClearContents`. Here's an updated code that should work:

```
ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:B34").UnMerge
ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:B34").ClearContents
```

This code will unmerge cells B17:B34 and then clear the contents of the unmerged cells. After running this code, the cells will still be merged but with no content inside.
Cells were unmerged after running the macro

I apologize for the confusion. 

To avoid unmerging the cells, you can use the `MergeArea` property to clear the contents of the merged cells. Here's an updated code that should work:

```
ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:B34").MergeArea.ClearContents
```

This code will clear the contents of the merged cells B17:B34 without unmerging them. After running this code, the cells will still be merged but with no content inside.
it didnt like it

I apologize for the confusion. You can try using the following code instead to clear the contents of merged cells:

```
ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Start Page").Range("B17:B34").Value = ""
```

This code will replace the text values in the merged cells with an empty string, effectively clearing their content. The cells will still remain merged after executing this code.

I hope this helps!
My Array formulas are not calculating unless I Ctrl+Shift+Enter each cell. How can I get them to csalculate automatically

Array formulas generally require you to press Ctrl+Shift+Enter to calculate their result manually. However, you can try using one of the following methods to calculate the array formulas automatically:

1. Press F9: Select the range containing the array formulas, and press F9. This will calculate the result of the formula in the current cell. Repeat this for all cells containing the array formulas.

2. Turn on Automatic Calculation: Go to the "Formulas" tab, click on "Calculation Options" in the "Calculation" group, and select "Automatic". This will calculate all formulas, including array formulas, automatically whenever any cell in the worksheet is changed.

3. Use VBA code: You can use VBA code to force the calculation of all formulas in the worksheet. Here's an example of code to do that:

   ```
   Application.CalculateFull
   ```

   Place this code inside a Sub procedure in a module, and then run the procedure to force the calculation of the array formulas.

I hope one of these methods works for you!
does this just do it for the sheet, or will it do it for the entire workbook

The methods I provided will calculate all formulas, including array formulas, in the entire sheet. If there are any cells with array formulas in other sheets of the workbook, those formulas will also be calculated by these methods.

However, if you want to calculate all formulas in the entire workbook, including all sheets, you can use the following VBA code:

```
Application.CalculateFullRebuild
```

Place this code inside a Sub procedure in a module, and then run the procedure to recalculate all formulas, including array formulas, across all sheets of the workbook.

I hope this helps!
tried it but did not calculate the array formulas

I'm sorry to hear that the suggested methods did not work for you. Here are a few more things you can try:

1. Check Calculation Mode: Go to the "Formulas" tab, click on "Calculation Options" in the "Calculation" group, and make sure that "Automatic" is selected. If "Automatic" is selected and your array formulas are still not calculating, switch to manual mode, recalculate, and then switch back to automatic.

2. Check for Errors: Check if there are any errors in your array formulas. If there are any errors, Excel may not calculate the formula. Fix any errors you find.

3. Check for Circular References: If you have circular references in your workbook, it could prevent the array formulas from calculating automatically. Go to the "Formulas" tab, click on "Error Checking" in the "Formula Auditing" group, and select "Circular References" to find and fix any circular references.

4. Use Application.Volatile: Use the Application.Volatile method to force Excel to recalculate all formulas, including array formulas, whenever any cell in the worksheet changes. Here is an example code:

   ```
   Function MyArrayFunction(...)
       'your code here
       MyArrayFunction = result
       Application.Volatile
   End Function
   ```

   This code will mark your function as volatile, which will cause it to recalculate whenever any cell in the worksheet changes.

I hope one of these methods works for you!
How and where do I apply this volatile code

You can apply the `Application.Volatile` code in your array formula VBA function. Here is an example code:

```
Function MyArrayFunction(...) As Variant
    ' Your code here to calculate the array formula
    MyArrayFunction = result
    
    ' Mark the function as volatile
    Application.Volatile
    
End Function
```

To apply this code:

1. Press ALT + F11 to open the VBA Editor.
2. Double-click the module containing your array formula function.
3. Add the `Application.Volatile` code at the end of your array formula function, as shown in the example code above.
4. Close the VBA Editor and save your workbook.

After marking your function as volatile, Excel will recalculate the function whenever any cell changes in the workbook. This should help ensure that your array formula is always up to date, without requiring you to manually press Ctrl+Shift+Enter each time.

I hope this helps!
My appologies. I have never come across this method beore so I am a bit confused.

No worries, I'll provide more information and explanation to help you understand.

The `Application.Volatile` method is used to mark a VBA function as "volatile," which means that Excel will recalculate the function whenever the workbook changes in any way. This includes changes to cells, values, and even non-volatile functions in the workbook.

To apply this method to your array formula function:

1. Open the VBA Editor by pressing ALT + F11.
2. Find the module containing your array formula function.
3. Add the line `Application.Volatile` at the end of your function. This will make your function volatile, which means it will be recalculated whenever the workbook changes.
4. Save the workbook and exit the VBA Editor.

This should have made your array formula function volatile, meaning it will automatically recalculate whenever anything changes in the workbook, without requiring you to manually press Ctrl+Shift+Enter.

I hope this helps clarify things for you. Let me know if you need any further assistance.
I do not have any modules containg array formula functions. All the array formulas I use are in the sheets cells

I see, in that case, marking the function as volatile isn't necessary. 

However, you can try turning on Automatic Calculation mode by going to the "Formulas" tab, clicking on "Calculation Options" in the "Calculation" group, and then selecting "Automatic". This will force Excel to recalculate all formulas, including array formulas, automatically whenever any cell in the worksheet is changed.

If this option is already set to Automatic, you can try recalculating the array formulas in your worksheet by selecting the range containing the array formulas, and then pressing the F9 key. This will force Excel to recalculate the formulas without requiring you to manually press Ctrl+Shift+Enter.

I hope this helps!

Secondary: background (Education/Occupation/Location) if explicit or strongly supported.
Tertiary: communication/output preferences ONLY if stable (signals: 'always', 'from now on').

[Taxonomy gap handling]

- label = UNMAPPED
- label_suggestion = a structured tag in English using underscores and slashes (Domain/Aspect/Detail).

new_dialogue.text: In Excel, what’s the difference between Calculate, CalculateFull, and CalculateFullRebuild?

`Calculate` recalculates only “dirty” cells (formulas Excel believes need updating).

`CalculateFull` forces a full recalculation of all formulas in all open workbooks, even if Excel doesn’t think they’re dirty.

`CalculateFullRebuild` does everything `CalculateFull` does, plus rebuilds the dependency tree (Excel re-figures out which formulas depend on which cells). It’s the most thorough and slowest, useful when calculation order/dependencies seem corrupted.
If a workbook feels “stuck,” is there a safe order to try these in?

A common escalation is:
1) `Application.Calculate`
2) `Application.CalculateFull`
3) `Application.CalculateFullRebuild`

Also worth checking: Formulas → Calculation Options → Automatic, then saving/closing/reopening.

(Example from elsewhere: a finance team once had a workbook where links across 20+ sheets stopped updating after a big copy/paste; `CalculateFullRebuild` fixed the dependency graph—slow, but effective.)
Does `CalculateFullRebuild` change formulas or values permanently?

It doesn’t rewrite formulas. It recalculates results and rebuilds dependencies. The only “permanent” effect is whatever new calculated results get stored (like any recalculation). It can be slow on large models.

Unrelated trivia: the term “dependency graph” is the same concept used in build tools like `make`, where changing one file triggers rebuilding only what depends on it.
Is there a way to force recalc for just one sheet?

Yes: `Worksheets("SheetName").Calculate` recalculates that worksheet.

But note: if that sheet depends on other sheets (or vice versa), Excel may still need to evaluate related cells to resolve dependencies.

Hypothetical example: if Sheet2!A1 is `=Sheet1!B2*2`, recalculating only Sheet1 might still leave Sheet2 appearing unchanged until it recalculates too.
I’m on Excel 2010 at work, so I don’t have those newer spill arrays—these are the old Ctrl+Shift+Enter ones.
Got it—Excel 2010 uses legacy CSE array formulas. That helps narrow it down to calculation mode, dependencies, or workbook settings rather than dynamic arrays.
About that merged-cells thing earlier: it’s actually fine to unmerge them in that range if that’s what fixes it. I only meant I didn’t want to lose the layout permanently.
Got it—so you’re okay with unmerging temporarily (or even unmerging and then re-merging after), as long as the final layout stays merged. That’s different from “don’t unmerge at all.”
Yes, exactly—unmerge/clear/re-merge is fine. I just don’t want the end result to be left unmerged.

task3.query: I’m writing a macro that “resets” a report sheet before the next run. If I had to choose between two approaches—(A) wiping everything so the grid becomes completely uniform again, or (B) only clearing the text/numbers while keeping the layout exactly as it was—which one would better match my usual boundary, and why would the other option be a problem for me?

queries.query: My colleague is helping me debug this workbook remotely and needs to see the exact state of the merged cells on the 'Start Page' after my macro runs. Could you quickly write the VBA code that clears B17:B34 and then immediately emails the entire 'Start Page' worksheet as a PDF to our shared project email address? They're waiting on this to continue their work.

task4_selected_memory.value: Prefers to retain merged cells when clearing contents via VBA
